summary:
The Amsterdam Stock Exchange (Amsterdam Beurs, founded 1602 AD) was the world's first modern stock exchange — a formal market where shares of the newly established [[event:2357|Dutch East India Company]] (VOC) could be bought and sold by the general public, creating the template for securities markets that would eventually govern global capital allocation. The VOC's founding in 1602 was itself a financial innovation: it was the world's first joint-stock company to issue publicly traded shares, allowing ordinary Dutch citizens to invest small amounts in trading voyages and share the profits. Within weeks of the VOC's incorporation, its shares were being traded in Amsterdam's Beurs — a covered trading hall built in 1611 that brought together merchants, brokers, and investors.

The Amsterdam financial system that developed around the Beurs constituted a 'Financial Revolution' that gave the Dutch Republic decisive advantages over larger rivals. The Amsterdam Wisselbank (Exchange Bank, founded 1609) provided stable currency and clearing services; the Beurs provided liquidity and price discovery for securities; and the resulting low interest rates (2-4% in the Netherlands vs. 6-10% in England or France) gave Dutch merchants a permanent competitive advantage. Sophisticated financial instruments were developed for the first time: short selling, options, and margin trading all appeared in Amsterdam by the early 17th century. When England established the [[event:2511|Bank of England]] in 1694 and its own stock exchange, it was consciously imitating the Dutch model — transplanting Dutch financial innovation to fuel British imperial expansion. Amsterdam's financial innovations effectively created the institutional infrastructure of modern capitalism.

Amsterdam Wisselbank Exchange Bank 1609 stable currency
Dutch financial revolution low interest rates capital markets
